results.possibleIdentity: Frank Hood (1912–1987) was an engineer, filmmaker, and inventor based in Portland, Oregon, who played a foundational role in the region's experimental and independent film community during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Originally a radio engineer and the 24th employee of the technology company Tektronix, he transitioned into filmmaking by producing training and promotional films. In 1964, he founded Teknifilm (also known as Teknifilm Labs), a processing facility that became a central hub and "gathering place" for Portland's independent filmmakers and experimental artists. Hood was notably associated with the local art and technology scene that intersected with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement; when Jack Eyerly established E.A.T. chapters in Portland and Seattle in 1968, Teknifilm served as a community anchor. Hood was known for his support of the arts, often providing film processing services on credit to help young and struggling artists. He was also a collector of antique movie cameras and projectors and is credited as a "writer-director" in the context of Oregon's film history. His son, George Hood, was also an experimental animator involved in the Portland E.A.T. happenings.
